content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Jonathan S. Fuhrman 
Compliance Consultant 
360 S. Euclid Avenue, #115 
Pasadena, CA 
91101 
Dear Mr. Fuhrman: 
July 21, 1988 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 88-265 
Your letter requesting advice concerning the recently 
enacted campaign finance reform initiatives (Government Code 
Section 85100, et ~) was received on July 12, 1988 by the 
Fair Political Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions 
about your advice request, you may contact Kathryn Donovan, an 
attorney in the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
• 
Because of the delayed operative date of most of the 
provisions of these initiatives, and the numerous inquiries we 
are receiving relative to implementation, we are attempting to 
respond to questions regarding interpretation in an organized 
and efficient fashion. 
Due to the volume of requests and 
complexity of the issues involved, we will answer your question 
in a timely manner, but not within the usual twenty-one working 
days. 
(Government Code section 83114 (b) . ) 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to information needed. 
You also should be aware 
that your letter and our response are public records which may 
be disclosed to the public upon receipt of a proper request for 
disclosure. 
GWB:plh:2173LTR 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804,0807 • (916)322,5660 


California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Jonathan S. Fuhrman 
compliance Consultant 
October 2, 1989 
360 S. Euclid Avenue, #115 
Pasadena, CA 
91101 
Dear Mr. Fuhrman: 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-88-265 
In June 1988, you first wrote to us concerning the effect of 
Government Code section 85306, as added by Proposition 73,11 
on 
the use of campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989. 
This 
topic has been the subject of litigation for the last several 
months. 
We have included your name on our mailing list for 
Propositions 68 and 73, and thereby attempted to keep you informed 
of the Commission's interpretation of the law during this time 
period. 
The purpose of this letter is to inform you of the most 
recent development on this issue. 
On September 14, 1989, a federal court ruled that section 
85306 is unconstitutional and enjoined the Fair Political 
Practices commission from enforcing that law. 
(Service Employees 
International Union v. Fair Political Practices Commission, Case 
No. CIVS 89-0433 LKK-JFM, U.S. District Court, Eastern District of 
California.) 
A copy of the court order is enclosed. 
Accordingly, campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989 
may now be used to support or oppose a candidacy for elective 
office. 
We caution that candidates and elected officers who 
possess those funds may not use them to make contributions to any 
other candidate for elective office. 
(Section 85304.) 
We also 
caution that these funds may not be used for personal 
expenditures, as defined in Elections Code Sections 12400-12407. 
Attached is a commission advice letter which explains the 
procedures for transferring these funds to a current campaign 
account. 
Proposition 73 was adopted by the voters in June 1988. 
amended the Political Reform Act (Government Code sections 
91015.) 
All references are to the Government Code unless 
otherwise indicated. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 •

California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Jonathan S. Fuhrman 
Compliance Consultant 
360 S. Euclid Ave., #115 
Pasadena, CA 
91101 
Dear Mr. Fuhrman: 
August 22, 1988 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-88-265 
You have asked several questions concerning the provisions 
of Proposition 73 which restrict the use of campaign funds 
commencing January 1, 1989. 
The specific statute in question 
is Governm~nt Code section 85306, which provides: 
Any person who possesses campaign funds on the 
effective date of this chapter may expend those funds for 
any lawful purpose other than to support or oppose 
candidacy. 
On September 8, 1988, the Fair Political Practices 
Commission will hold a hearing to adopt emergency regulations 
to implement section 85306. 
The hearing will commence at 
9:00 a.m., at the Commission offices, 428 J Street, suite 800, 
Sacramento. 
We anticipate that the emergency regulations 
adopted at this hearing will determine the answers to most of 
the questions presented in your letter. 
The Commission has directed its staff to prepare draft 
regulations covering the possible alternative interpretations, 
and to recommend which alternatives the Commission should adopt 
as emergency regulations at the September 8 meeting. 
These 
draft alternative regulations and staff recommendations will be 
sent to you and other interested persons approximately one week 
before the Commission meeting. 
You are invited to attend the 
Commission meeting and you will be afforded an opportunity to 
present comments to the Commission. 
We are creating a list of persons who plan to testify at 
the September 8 Commission meeting. 
Please contact Jeanette 
Turvill at (916) 322-5901 not later than September 7 if you 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 •

We will make every effort to 
permit all interested persons an opportunity to testify; 
however, first priority will be granted to those persons on the 
list.

Sincerely,

i/' 
1/,,--
By: 
KathrYn E. Donovan 
Counsel, Legal Division 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
Sacramento, Ca. 
Dear Sirs: 
360 S. Euclid Ave., #115 
Pasadena, Ca. 91101 
Ju ne 30, 1988 
I am writing on behalf of Assemblywoman Lucille Roybal Allard to request a formal 
opinion on the following questions dealing with Prop. 73. 
1) A campaign committee controlled by Assemblywoman Allard has purchased 
various assets, specifically office equipment and computers, with funds collected 
prior to January 1, 1989. May the committee continue to use such assets after Janu-
ary 1, 19897 
2) A campaign committee controlled by Assemblywoman Allard intends to rent 
new office space this fall. A security deposit and the last month's rent will be re-
quired before entering into the lease. Further, the rent is to be paid in advance, with 
rent for the next month due on or before the last day of the prior month. First, may 
the committee pay the security deposit and last month's rent with funds collected 
prior to January 1, 1989, and continue to occupy the office after January 1, 19897 
Secondly, may the committee pay the rent for January, 1989, on or before December 
31, 1988, as contractually required, using funds collected prior to January 1, 19897 
Finally, may the committee pre-pay one year's rent, and thus possibly obtain a sig-
nificant discount on the total rent due, with funds collected prior to January 1,19897 
We realize that interpreting the provisions of Prop. 73 has become a significant bur-
den on the Commission. Nonetheless, Assemblywoman Allard and I are hoping for 
an early response to these questions. 
Thank you very much for your assistance. 
Sincerely yours, 
Jonathan S. Fuhrman 
Compliance Consultant

sections.question: 1) A campaign committee controlled by Assemblywoman Allard has purchased various assets, specifically office equipment and computers, with funds collected prior to January 1, 1989. May the committee continue to use such assets after January 1, 1989? 2) A campaign committee controlled by Assemblywoman Allard intends to rent new office space this fall. A security deposit and the last month's rent will be required before entering into the lease. Further, the rent is to be paid in advance, with rent for the next month due on or before the last day of the prior month. First, may the committee pay the security deposit and last month's rent with funds collected prior to January 1, 1989, and continue to occupy the office after January 1, 1989? Secondly, may the committee pay the rent for January, 1989, on or before December 31, 1988, as contractually required, using funds collected prior to January 1, 1989? Finally, may the pre-pay one year's rent, and thus possibly obtain a significant discount on the total rent due, with funds collected prior to January 1, 1989?
sections.conclusion: Campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989 may now be used to support or oppose a candidacy for elective office. Candidates and elected officers who possess those funds may not use them to make contributions to any other candidate for elective office (Section 85304). These funds may not be used for personal expenditures, as defined in Elections Code Sections 12400-12407.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks whether a campaign committee controlled by Assemblywoman Lucille Roybal Allard may continue using assets purchased with pre-1989 campaign funds after January 1, 1989, and whether pre-1989 funds may be used to pay office rental deposits, advance rent payments, and prepaid annual rent under Proposition 73.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Following a federal court ruling that invalidated Government Code section 85306 as unconstitutional, pre-January 1, 1989 campaign funds may be used to support or oppose candidacy, but not for contributions to other candidates or for personal expenditures.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of multiple letters spanning June 1988 to October 1989. The initial incoming request from Jonathan S. Fuhrman is followed by three FPPC responses. The August 22, 1988 response indicates answers would follow emergency regulations. The October 2, 1989 response provides the final FPPC conclusion based on a federal court ruling. Some specific section numbers are referenced but full detailed legal analysis is not fully explicit in the October 1989 letter.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1) A campaign committee controlled by Assemblywoman Allard has purchased various assets, specifically office equipment and computers, with funds collected prior to January 1, 1989. May the committee continue to use such assets after January 1, 1989? 2) A campaign committee controlled by Assemblywoman Allard intends to rent new office space this fall. A security deposit and the last month's rent will be required before entering into the lease. Further, the rent is to be paid in advance, with rent for the next month due on or before the last day of the prior month. First, may the committee pay the security deposit and last month's rent with funds collected prior to January 1, 1989, and continue to occupy the office after January 1, 1989? Secondly, may the committee pay the rent for January, 1989, on or before December 31, 1988, as contractually required, using funds collected prior to January 1, 1989? Finally, may the pre-pay one year's rent, and thus possibly obtain a significant discount on the total rent due, with funds collected prior to January 1, 1989?

CONCLUSION: Campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989 may now be used to support or oppose a candidacy for elective office. Candidates and elected officers who possess those funds may not use them to make contributions to any other candidate for elective office (Section 85304). These funds may not be used for personal expenditures, as defined in Elections Code Sections 12400-12407.

embedding.summary: This file contains the initial advice request from June 1988 regarding Proposition 73's restrictions on pre-1989 campaign funds, followed by FPPC responses indicating the Commission would address the questions through emergency regulations and subsequently notifying the requester of a federal court ruling that struck down the restrictive provision as unconstitutional.
